video_title: The Strategy That Thrives When Markets Panic | Systematic Investor | Ep.403
gist: Efficient Capital's Marat Molyboga joins Katy Kaminski and Niels Kaastrup-Larsen to argue trend following/managed futures is best understood as the 'second responder' in a risk-mitigation framework, that geopolitical-risk shocks drive inflation and therefore profitable commodity and fixed-income trends, and that short-term trend can boost crisis alpha but only with heavy execution investment. They also dismiss mean-variance optimization in favor of equal-risk diversification and review a 2026 trend-following environment marked by a geopolitical (Iran) shock and 'higher-for-longer' fixed-income uncertainty.

insights.claim: Through May 2026, trend-following indices are tightly clustered and positive year-to-date: the BTOP50, SG CTA and SG Trend indices are each up roughly 10.25-10.5%, and the short-term traders index up about 5.3%.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Confirms a broad, low-dispersion positive CTA year so far, useful baseline for allocators sizing managed-futures exposure into mid-2026.

insights.claim: 2026 has featured trends morphing across regimes: a Q1 risk-on equity/FX trade, then a geopolitical shock from the Iran conflict that sparked a strong energy/inflation-uncertainty trend (the strongest since 2022), followed by equity trends returning in April.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest, AlphaSimplex)
insights.so_what: Shows trend strategies capturing rotating themes; energy/commodity trends re-emerged as a major 2026 driver after years of dormancy.
insights.quote: one of the big trends we haven't seen since uh 2022

insights.claim: Current trends are unusually offsetting ('hedged'): an AI-growth equity trend coexists with short fixed-income (higher-for-longer) and long energy/base-metals positions, alongside an atypical correlation structure where energy is not its usual negative correlation to equities.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: An unusual correlation regime that trend thrives on but that can mute net portfolio P&L; watch for the correlation structure normalizing.
insights.quote: it feels a little bit like a hedge trend environment like they're a little bit offsetting

insights.claim: Fixed income is the hardest asset class for trend in 2026 because the Fed has shifted from expectations of multiple rate cuts six to seven months ago toward potentially hiking due to inflation concerns, creating whipsaws.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest, Efficient Capital)
insights.so_what: Signals continued stop-start losses in rates books; allocators should not expect bonds to be a clean trend contributor near-term.
insights.quote: now the Fed is talking about potential even hiking rates because of inflationary concerns

insights.claim: The managed-futures industry suffered its worst-ever drawdown in June of last year (2025) but has staged a strong recovery since July 2025, renewing institutional interest in larger managed-futures allocations.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: The post-drawdown rebound plus rising allocator interest is a classic late-to-the-party flow setup worth tracking for crowding.
insights.quote: if you look at June of last year as an industry, we experienced the worst draw down ever

insights.claim: In May 2026, short-term managers were the best performers in the CTA universe while long-term trend and diversifying managers struggled relatively, a reversal from 2025 when short-term managers had a bad year.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Sub-strategy leadership rotation argues for diversifying across CTA styles rather than chasing last year's winner.

insights.claim: Efficient Capital segments the CTA universe into three groups - long-term trend followers, short-term managers, and diversifying strategies - that behave very differently across environments, though in 2026 all three are performing similarly, mainly aided by equities and commodities.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: A practical taxonomy for portfolio construction; convergent 2026 behavior reduces the diversification benefit between groups this year.

insights.claim: Trend following is best framed as the 'second responder' in MEKETA's risk-mitigation framework: first responders (tail-risk/long-vol) react to sudden 10-15% equity drops, while second-responder CTAs benefit from prolonged dislocations lasting quarters to years.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Sets correct expectations: don't judge CTAs on a one-week selloff; they are for protracted regime change, complementing tail-risk hedges.
insights.quote: investors get so disappointed with CTAs where they don't perform great during the weekly market selloff but they're not designed for that

insights.claim: Despite attractive long-term returns and unique crisis alpha, managed-futures AUM remains small relative to other hedge-fund strategies because investors misunderstand trend's portfolio role, performance-chase, and misjudge how to access the asset class.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Identifies the structural under-allocation gap; closing these three obstacles is the bull case for asset-class growth.

insights.claim: Performance chasing at the manager level is a poor strategy because CTA return persistence is low; a paper by Efficient's Joel Handy and Lauren Mexie in the Journal of Wealth Management showed picking the largest/best 3-year managers from the SG index constituents produces poor results.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Empirical caution against ranking-and-picking top recent CTAs; favors diversified multi-manager selection.

insights.claim: Investors systematically performance-chase at the industry level: they get excited about CTAs after a crisis, take a year or two to allocate into a now-normal environment with only okay returns, get disappointed, and de-allocate right before the next crisis.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Describes the recurring mistiming cycle; contrarian allocators should add to CTAs during calm post-allocation lulls, not after crises.
insights.quote: they decide to deallocate right before the next crisis

insights.claim: A diversified multi-manager approach accessed through managed accounts is the optimal way to hold managed futures, because in 2022 the dispersion between the best and worst SG CTA index manager was about 80% even though the asset class was up over 20%.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Quantifies idiosyncratic single-manager risk; a single 2022 manager could lose 30%+ while the class gained 20%, arguing for diversification.
insights.quote: that return dispersion was around 80%

insights.claim: Managed accounts let investors scale high-Sharpe portfolios up to hit target returns at a desired volatility, unlike typical funds-of-hedge-funds that deliver great Sharpe but cannot be levered to attractive absolute returns.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Highlights the scalability advantage of the managed-account structure for institutions targeting specific return/vol levels.

insights.claim: Mean-variance optimization is 'a beautiful theory with ugly results' that fails in practice because it depends on predicting expected returns; out-of-sample performance is poor and portfolio weights are unstable.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Pushes allocators away from optimizer-driven weights toward more robust heuristics for real-money portfolios.
insights.quote: I call minus optimization a beautiful theory with ugly results

insights.claim: Equal-risk allocation, intelligently tweaked for strategy clusters via top-down frameworks, is more promising than any mean-variance extension because diversification is the only 'free lunch' in finance.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: A concrete, robustness-first construction recipe institutions can adopt over optimizer outputs.

insights.claim: Diversifying across the dimension of time via volatility-based position sizing (vol targeting) at the portfolio level, not just at the strategy level, can further improve risk-adjusted performance.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Argues vol-targeting is a portfolio-level alpha source, not merely a strategy-level risk control.

insights.claim: The roughly-2000 operations-research '1/N' paper showed naive equal weighting beats complex portfolio-choice models because estimation uncertainty in finance is too high for the precision those models require.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Academic backing for simple diversification; estimate only what you can (correlations, volatility) and use it with caution.
insights.quote: uncertainty in finance is too high for the complexity and the precision that we have to estimate a lot of these things

insights.claim: QIS (quantitative investment strategies) products are more prone to overfitting than asset managers because banks offer hundreds or thousands of strategies with no reputational stake, whereas an asset manager must stamp approval and put reputation on the line.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: A skeptical lens on bank QIS offerings; reputational alignment is a screening criterion when choosing strategy providers.
insights.quote: the reputation is not on the line. Therefore, I think that QS strategist are more much more susceptible to overfitting issues

insights.claim: Being 'approximately right' with well-reasoned decisions beats being 'precisely wrong' with the nerdiest formula - a core mindset distinguishing practical CTA portfolio management from academic precision.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: A guiding principle that favors robust, simple approaches over false precision in systematic investing.
insights.quote: you can get the nerdiest formula and do everything uh but you'll be precisely wrong

insights.claim: Academic geopolitical-risk indices, built from frequency of GPR terms in print media (e.g., Wall Street Journal, Washington Post) and now LLM/AI classification, are publicly downloadable and granular by country, oil-vs-other, and threat-vs-realized-action.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Investors can directly download and monitor GPR data to time/contextualize regime shifts and inflation risk.
insights.quote: this data is very granular. It's not just a series. It covers all different countries around the globe

insights.claim: Higher geopolitical risk historically raises perceived disaster probability, increases risk aversion (making risky assets seem riskier), and lowers consumer confidence, potentially reducing growth.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames the transmission channels by which GPR spikes reprice risk assets and dent growth.

insights.claim: A January 2026 paper (Cavallo with three co-authors) found that increases in geopolitical risk are often followed by two to three years of elevated inflation, plus lower growth, supply disruptions, higher military spending, money-supply growth, and reduced international trade.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Provides the mechanistic link - GPR leads to multi-year inflation - that explains why trend profits during geopolitical stress; supports a multi-year inflation-tilt thesis.
insights.quote: geopolitical risk and increases in geopolitical risk is often followed by an increase in inflation

insights.claim: During the largest increases in geopolitical risk since SG Trend's 2000 inception, equities and fixed income tended to fall, energy spiked, and the US dollar rose - a pattern consistent with an incoming inflation shock.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Gives the asset-class playbook for GPR spikes; note dollar/energy signs may differ over longer histories than the 25-year window.

insights.claim: Managed futures delivered strongly positive returns during periods of high geopolitical risk, while hedge funds struggled (consistent with beta expansion) and equity market neutral was roughly flat.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Empirical case for managed futures as a geopolitical-risk hedge, distinct from broad hedge-fund exposure.

insights.claim: Managed futures exhibits a 'CTA smile' across geopolitical-risk states: flat when nothing is changing, but better when GPR is rising or falling sharply - reinforcing it as a change/crisis-alpha strategy rather than an equity-timing play.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Sets expectations that CTAs add value at regime inflections, not in calm markets - guides when to lean into the allocation.
insights.quote: you actually see that CTA smile

insights.claim: Equities are not a major trend contributor during extreme geopolitical moves; commodities and fixed income drive the big moves because GPR raises inflation probability and commodity supply shocks are widely shared.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Timing equities through geopolitical events is hard; commodity and rate trends are the more reliable GPR-driven P&L source.
insights.quote: timing the equity moves during these geopolitical events is hard

insights.claim: Whether a geopolitical shock hits equities depends on whether it impairs growth; the current (Iran) shock has been an energy shock that has not yet hit growth, unlike the Ukraine invasion which was more a gas/food shock.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Explains why 2026 equities held up despite conflict; watch for the shock spreading to growth as the trigger for an equity downtrend.

insights.claim: Efficient Capital's earlier research cautioning against over-relying on the 60/40 portfolio found that in high-inflation historical regimes, stocks and bonds delivered mixed results while long-only commodities and trend following stood out as the two best performers.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Reinforces commodity exposure and trend as the inflation-regime diversifiers when the 60/40 negative correlation breaks down.

insights.claim: Geopolitical-risk effects on trend may be understated at monthly frequency; longer quarterly or annual horizons could show stronger trend results because new geopolitically-induced trends take time to develop and be captured.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Suggests evaluating trend's GPR benefit over longer horizons; a research gap that may underrate the strategy's protection.

insights.claim: Adding short-term trend to long- and medium-term trend improves long-term performance without sacrificing crisis alpha, per Efficient Capital's Journal of Portfolio Management paper and a 2026 follow-up.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Supports allocating a sleeve to short-term trend to lift returns while preserving the crisis-hedge that justifies the asset class.

insights.claim: Short-term trend boosts crisis alpha by offsetting losses long-term trend suffers during early transition periods, such as February 2020's rapid COVID reversal when slow trend struggled but short-term trend thrived.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the mechanism (covering early-transition gaps) for combining time horizons to smooth crisis performance.

insights.claim: Short-term trend's edge is extremely sensitive to implementation: without investment in colocated servers and specialized execution algos, transaction and market-impact costs quickly erase the advantage.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Execution infrastructure is the gating factor; allocators should vet a short-term manager's execution stack, not just signals.
insights.quote: if you don't invest in execution... the edge that the short-term trend can provide can quickly go away

insights.claim: The more a manager deviates from long-term trend following, the higher its 'mortality' (failure rate): short-term managers fail more than long-term trend followers, and diversifying strategies fail even more.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Manager-selection difficulty rises sharply away from classic trend; expect higher attrition and selection risk in short-term and diversifying sleeves.
insights.quote: the more different managers are from being long-term trend following, the higher their mortality is going to be

insights.claim: Successful short-term traders must constantly innovate beyond pure short-term trend - using techniques like pattern recognition - because pure short-term trend signals decay and the strategy is so hard to monetize.
insights.speaker: Marat Molyboga (guest)
insights.so_what: Short-term 'trend' allocations are often heterogeneous, non-trend strategies; due diligence should probe what is actually generating returns.

insights.claim: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen argues basic trend models with varied look-back periods tend to lose money over time, so he doubts pure short-term trend 'following' is profitable and suspects post-COVID short-term success came from trading volatility expansion rather than following.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: A practitioner caution that 'short-term trend' labels may mask other mechanisms; question whether following per se is the edge.

insights.claim: Short-term and long-vol strategies that became popular right after COVID have underperformed over the last five years with AUM adjusting down, possibly because crises have changed - 2022 was a slow crisis and very short V-shaped shocks (like a 'liberation day') hurt short-term managers too.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Implies very slow, replicator-style trend may handle ultra-fast V-shaped reversals better than short-term managers; matters for sleeve selection.
insights.quote: if the crisis is too short, right? Like a V-shaped rec... then the short-term guys can't handle it either

insights.claim: Market microstructure has shifted so that a market opening higher used to finish higher about 64% of the time but now closes higher only roughly 56-58%, materially hurting short-term breakout strategies.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host), citing Toby Crabel
insights.so_what: Quantifies decay in the open-breakout edge; a structural headwind for intraday breakout CTAs worth weighing in allocations.
insights.quote: when a market opened higher it usually finished higher like 64% of the time... and now he says it's like 56% or 58%

insights.claim: Reactivity (faster trend following) helps in certain crises like COVID but on average it is valuable to hold a toolkit of speeds, since a very slow trend follower would have had a harder time in 2020.
insights.speaker: Katy Kaminski (guest)
insights.so_what: Argues for multi-speed trend diversification rather than a single horizon, matching different crisis shapes.

insights.claim: Amazon's Alexa-based voice AI shopping assistant is reportedly converting shoppers about 3.5x better than traditional search, signaling AI-driven changes in e-commerce conversion.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Anecdotal data point on AI commercialization in retail; watch conversion-lift claims as an AI monetization signal.

insights.claim: Google has shifted from doing stock buybacks to issuing $80 billion of stock to fund AI capex, reversing a key support for equity markets.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: If big-tech buybacks give way to equity issuance for AI capex, a structural equity-market support could weaken.

insights.claim: For the first time ever, wind and solar generated more electricity worldwide than natural gas in the month of April.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Milestone in the energy transition with potential long-run implications for natural-gas demand trends.
insights.quote: for the first time ever wind and solar generated more electricity worldwide than natural gas

insights.claim: Ten companies now make up 40% of the S&P 500, per a new passive-investing paper Niels discussed with Harley Bassman, Mike Green and a co-author, underscoring extreme index concentration.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Extreme concentration heightens passive-flow fragility risk; relevant to anyone holding cap-weighted index exposure.
insights.quote: 10 companies in the S&P 500 uh make up 40% of the index
